subject: Product Management
title: Choosing the Right Metric
description: A quick, practical guide to Choosing the Right Metric.

hook.text: A team A/B tested a new checkout flow. Clicks increased 20%. But revenue dropped 5%. They were measuring the wrong metric.

buildup.text: Every experiment needs a primary metric (what you're trying to improve) and guardrail metrics (what you don't want to break).

discovery.text: Primary metrics should be close to the change: button click rate for a button test, not revenue. Revenue is too far downstream.

twist.text: Guardrail metrics catch side effects: page load time, error rates, support tickets, and adjacent feature usage.

climax.text: The best experiments define metrics before the test starts. Choosing metrics after seeing results is cherry-picking.

punchline.text: Define success before you test. Otherwise you'll find what you want to see.

quiz.question: Why should experiment metrics be defined before the test starts?
options: Choosing metrics after seeing results is cherry-picking — a bias that invalidates the experiment
Pre-defined metrics make the test run faster
Metrics defined after the test are always more accurate
